name: Burguillo Reservoir Loop via El Barraco and Navalmoral
name_original: Por el Embalse del Burguillo y el Berraco — circular por Navaluengo
description: Cycle 29.2 miles (46.9 km) around Embalse del Burguillo and El Berraco from Navaluengo, featuring scenic views and challenging climbs.
region_intro: Road cycling around Burgohondo is situated in the Alto Alberche valley, nestled between the Sierra de Gredos and Sierra de la Paramera. The region features a diverse landscape, transitioning from rugged granite outcrops to fertile meadows and forests, shaped by the Alberche River. Cyclists encounter significant elevation changes, including mountain passes like Puerto de Mijares and Puerto de Navalmoral, offering varied terrain for road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Burgohondo The most popular road cycling route is By the Burguillo Reservoir and the Berraco — circular by Navaluengo, a 29.1 miles (46.9 km) trail that takes 2 hours 12 minutes to complete, offering views of the Burguillo Reservoir. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Burguillo Reservoir – Puerto de Mijares loop from Parking del mirador estelar de Navaluenga, a difficult 77.8 miles (125.2 km) path, featuring substantial elevation gain through mountainous terrain. Local road cyclists also love the Puerto de Navalmoral – Convento de San José loop from Parking del mirador estelar de Navaluenga, a 49.2 miles (79.1 km) trail leading through the historic Puerto de Navalmoral, often completed in about 4 hours 6 minutes. Road cycling around Burgohondo is defined by the Alberche River valley, the foothills of the Sierra de Gredos, and varied granite landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging climbs. The routes in Burgohondo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 1 review. More than 750 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Burgohondo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Burgohondo?
A: Road cycling around Burgohondo offers a diverse landscape, transitioning from rugged granite outcrops to fertile meadows, groves, and forests. The routes are shaped by the Alberche River valley and the foothills of the Sierra de Gredos and Sierra de la Paramera, providing varied terrain with significant elevation changes, including challenging mountain passes like Puerto de Mijares and Puerto de Navalmoral.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Burgohondo?
A: Yes, Burgohondo offers a range of road cycling routes for different ability levels. While many routes feature significant elevation changes, there are options for easier rides with less than 1,000 feet of elevation gain. For a moderate, family-friendly option, consider &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1063075869/by-the-burguillo-reservoir-and-the-berraco-circular-by-navaluengo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;By the Burguillo Reservoir and the Berraco — circular by Navaluengo&lt;/a&gt;, which is 29.1 miles (46.9 km) long and offers scenic views.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling climbs in the Burgohondo region?
A: The Burgohondo region is known for its challenging climbs and mountain passes. Notable ascents include Puerto de Mijares and Puerto de Navalmoral, which has a history in world cycling, having been featured in the Vuelta España. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31879341" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Burguillo Reservoir – Puerto de Mijares loop from Parking del mirador estelar de Navaluenga&lt;/a&gt; feature substantial elevation gain, exceeding 2,200 meters over 125 km.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Burgohondo?
A: The region is particularly popular for cycling in March, benefiting from a more benevolent climate in the foothills of Gredos. Generally, spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures for road cycling. While some routes can be enjoyed year-round, it's always advisable to check local weather conditions, especially for higher passes, during winter months.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Burgohondo?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes in Burgohondo are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1063075869/by-the-burguillo-reservoir-and-the-berraco-circular-by-navaluengo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;By the Burguillo Reservoir and the Berraco — circular by Navaluengo&lt;/a&gt; and the more challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20144920" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Navalmoral – Convento de San José loop from Parking del mirador estelar de Navaluenga&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Where can I park my car when going road cycling in Burgohondo?
A: Many road cycling routes in the area, including several popular loops, often start from designated parking areas such as the Parking del mirador estelar de Navaluenga. This provides a convenient base for exploring the region's routes. Look for specific parking information on individual route descriptions.

Q: What natural attractions can I see along the road cycling routes in Burgohondo?
A: The routes often traverse areas rich in natural beauty. You can expect views of the Alberche River, which carves through the valley, and the surrounding mountain ranges of Sierra de Gredos and Sierra de la Paramera. Keep an eye out for unique granite landscapes, such as those near &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839745" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Zapatero&lt;/a&gt;, and diverse vegetation including alders, ash trees, and oaks. Some routes may pass near natural pools like Tabla de Abades or Puente Arco.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks to explore near the cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. Burgohondo itself is home to the 12th-century Abadía de Santa María, a significant historical monument. Nearby, you can find the Ermita de la Vera Cruz (Ermita de los Judíos) with its 15th-century frescoes. Some routes might take you through or near traditional towns like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5746092" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villanueva de Ávila&lt;/a&gt;, known for its architecture, or past historical structures like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4268792" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente Viejo de Navaluenga&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any waterfalls or wild swimming spots accessible from the road cycling routes?
A: While road cycling routes primarily focus on paved roads, the Alberche River, central to the region, offers several natural pools suitable for swimming, such as Tabla de Abades, Puente Arco, and El Cañal. Some natural monuments guides in the area mention waterfalls like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4112342" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Chorro Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6370654" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gavilanes&lt;/a&gt;, which might be a short detour from certain cycling paths.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Burgohondo?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from challenging mountain passes to scenic river valleys, and the opportunity to explore diverse landscapes. The combination of natural beauty and well-maintained routes makes it a popular destination for road cycling enthusiasts.

Q: Are there specific permits required for road cycling in the Burgohondo area or Sierra de Gredos?
A: Generally, no specific permits are required for road cycling on public roads in the Burgohondo area or within the Sierra de Gredos. However, it's always good practice to respect local regulations, especially when cycling through natural parks or protected areas. Always stay on marked roads and trails.

Q: Can I find cafes or pubs along the road cycling routes in Burgohondo?
A: While specific establishments are not listed in route details, the region has towns and villages like Burgohondo and Navaluenga where you can find cafes, restaurants, and pubs to refuel. Many routes pass through or near these settlements, offering opportunities for a break and refreshments.

faqs.question: How challenging is this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is considered a hard road ride requiring very good fitness. While Komoot classifies it as moderate, the terrain includes significant elevation changes and steep sections, with some short ramps reaching an 11% gradient. It's best suited for experienced road cyclists looking for a demanding and rewarding experience.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features well-paved surfaces, ideal for race bikes. However, be prepared for some segments that may be unpaved and more challenging to ride. There are numerous steep sections throughout the ride.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll cycle past the scenic Embalse del Burguillo, offering exceptional views with the Sierra de Gredos in the background. The route also passes through or near the settlement of El Berraco, and the wider area includes the Valle de Iruelas Natural Park and the magnificent Castañar del Tiemblo (chestnut forest).

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route in Navaluengo?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas for this exact route are not detailed, Navaluengo generally offers parking options suitable for cyclists. Look for public parking within the town to access the starting point conveniently.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The region is popular for cycling due to its challenging climbs and picturesque landscapes. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant weather conditions for tackling the significant elevation changes, avoiding the extreme heat of summer or potential colder conditions in winter.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with several other paths. You'll find yourself on sections of trails such as El Tejar, La Cendra del Espino, Los Bonales, and Camino de la Peguera, among others, for short distances.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, and while dogs are generally not suitable companions for race bike rides, if you are considering bringing a dog for other activities in the area, please check local regulations for specific parks or natural areas you might visit off the main route.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this public road route around the Burguillo Reservoir and El Berraco.

faqs.question: Are there any viewpoints along the route?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. The area around the Embalse del Burguillo is renowned for its exceptional views, particularly of the reservoir itself with the majestic Sierra de Gredos in the background. Keep an eye out for scenic spots as you ride.

faqs.question: What should I pack for this road cycling trip?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and elevation, pack essential cycling gear including appropriate clothing for varying weather, sufficient water and nutrition, a repair kit, and a fully charged phone. Due to the challenging nature, consider bringing extra layers and being prepared for steep sections.

faqs.question: Is public transport available to Navaluengo?
faqs.answer: Yes, Navaluengo is accessible by public transport, primarily bus services from larger nearby towns or cities. It's advisable to check current timetables for routes connecting to Navaluengo if you plan to arrive without a car.
